request_details.nature_of_emergency_and_amount_of_suppor:
Over winter break my mother, who is my primary financial supporter, was laid off from her job at a small manufacturing company in Chicago. She had been working there for over 8 years, and the company downsized unexpectedly right after New Year’s. Because of this, the small amount of money she usually sends me each month to help cover food and personal expenses completely stopped in January. We are still waiting to hear back about her unemployment benefits, and in the meantime she is behind on rent and utilities at home, so she is prioritizing keeping a roof over my younger sister’s head.

I came back to campus this spring with very limited funds. After buying textbooks and basic supplies, I realized I did not have enough money left to purchase a full meal plan or reliably buy groceries. For the first two weeks of the semester, I have been skipping breakfast most days and sometimes only eating one real meal a day, usually something cheap like instant ramen or peanut butter sandwiches. On days when I have late labs and programming group meetings, I’ve noticed I feel lightheaded and have trouble focusing because I haven’t eaten properly.

I am requesting 60 meal swipes to help cover my food needs for the remainder of the semester. I calculated this number based on using approximately 4–5 dining hall meals per week for the next 12–14 weeks, supplementing with the small amount of groceries I can afford using my part-time job income. Having 60 swipes would not be excessive, but it would allow me to reliably eat at least one balanced dining hall meal on the majority of days, especially on my longest class and work days. This would help stabilize my food situation while my family’s income is uncertain and until I can hopefully pick up a few more work hours on campus.
request_details.how_this_meal_plan_scholarship_would_ass:
Receiving this Meal Plan Scholarship would directly address the most immediate impact of my family’s financial crisis: consistent access to nutritious food. Right now, the stress of not knowing if I can afford to eat a full meal is affecting my ability to concentrate in class and stay engaged in my coursework. On multiple occasions in the last two weeks, I have sat in my afternoon algorithms lecture thinking more about how hungry I was and how I would afford dinner than about what the professor was explaining. As a Computer Science major taking 16 credits, including two programming-intensive courses and a lab, I need to be mentally sharp and able to focus for long stretches of time.

Having meal swipes would mean I could go to the dining halls without doing constant mental math about my bank account balance. Instead of stretching a few cheap groceries into unhealthy meals, I could rely on at least one solid, balanced meal a day with protein, vegetables, and fresh fruit. This would improve my energy levels, my sleep, and my ability to participate in group projects and late-night study sessions. It would also help stabilize my mood—right now I feel constantly anxious and guilty when I eat, because I’m picturing my mom and little sister back home trying to make ends meet.

This support would also reduce the pressure I feel to pick up as many extra work shifts as possible at the expense of my academics. I currently work part-time at the campus IT Help Center, and I’ve been tempted to ask for significantly more hours to cover food. However, that would likely mean staying late multiple nights a week and cutting into the time I need for homework, coding assignments, and exam preparation. With a meal plan scholarship covering a portion of my food needs, I could keep my work hours at a sustainable level and not risk falling behind in my classes.

Finally, this scholarship would give my family some emotional relief. My mother has expressed a lot of guilt that she cannot help me with food right now, on top of worrying about keeping her own household stable for my sister. If I can tell her that I have support from the university to make sure I can eat and stay healthy, it would take a significant emotional burden off both of us. In short, this scholarship would turn a very unstable and stressful situation into something manageable, allowing me to stay focused on my long-term goal of graduating on time and being able to support my family in the future.
request_details.efforts_made_to_procure_support_from_oth:
Since realizing that I would not be able to afford a full meal plan this semester, I have taken several steps to try to secure support from other resources before applying for this scholarship.

1. **Financial Aid Office:**
I contacted the UMass Financial Aid Office in mid-January as soon as my mom told me about her layoff. I spoke with a financial aid counselor over the phone and then followed up with an in-person appointment during the first week of classes. I explained the change in my family’s income and provided a copy of my mom’s layoff notice. The counselor said that they could submit a special circumstances review for my financial aid package, but that any adjustments would likely not occur until later in the semester and would probably affect next year’s aid more than this current term. They encouraged me to apply for emergency grants and referred me to the Dean of Students Office and the Student Success office.

2. **Emergency Funds and Grants:**
I completed an application for the UMass Student Emergency Fund, explaining our situation and attaching documentation. I am still waiting for a decision and understand that those funds, if approved, may need to be used for multiple expenses (like textbooks and personal necessities), not just food. Because I know those funds are limited and in high demand, I am trying not to rely on them alone for meals.

3. **Campus Food Pantry / Food Assistance:**
I learned about the UMass Food Pantry (or equivalent campus food assistance program) from a flyer in my residence hall and have already visited twice. I picked up shelf-stable items like rice, canned beans, pasta, and oatmeal. While this has helped me stretch my budget, the pantry hours and selection make it difficult to rely on for all of my meals, and it doesn’t fully cover fresh items like fruits and vegetables. I plan to continue using this resource responsibly, but I recognize it is meant to be a supplement, not a complete solution.

4. **Employment and Extra Work Hours:**
I currently work about 10–12 hours per week at the campus IT Help Center. I spoke with my supervisor about the possibility of picking up a few additional shifts. They were understanding but explained that there are limits because they need to distribute hours fairly among student employees. They offered to give me priority for open shifts when possible, and I have already picked up two extra shifts this month. However, I am cautious about significantly increasing my work hours, because my course load is heavy this semester, and I don’t want to jeopardize my grades and academic standing.

5. **Family and Community Support:**
I talked with my extended family (an aunt and an older cousin) about the situation. They were supportive emotionally but are also facing their own financial challenges and could only send very small amounts (like $20–$30) once, which mostly went toward textbook costs. I also looked into local community food resources in Amherst, but many are not easily accessible without a car, and I do not want to take away resources from permanent residents with families to support.

6. **Budgeting and Cost-Cutting:**
On my own, I have gone through my bank statements and cut out any non-essential spending. I cancelled a couple of small subscriptions (like a music streaming premium plan) and have been extremely careful about buying only basic groceries. I cook simple, low-cost meals in the residence hall kitchen when possible, but I am limited by shared kitchen access and the time it takes to cook from scratch on days when I have late labs and group projects.

After taking all of these steps, I still find that there is a gap between what I can realistically afford and what I need to maintain consistent, healthy meals. That is why I am applying specifically for this Meal Plan Scholarship—to fill that remaining gap in a targeted way so I can focus on my academics and well-being while my family works through this difficult period.
